comment:
New baker here, happy that people are understanding that bread can be made at home easily. I'll put down the easiest recipe you can have.
500g flour
330ml water
10g yeast
10g salt
20g olive oil
Mix flour and water in a bowl and let it rest 30 to 45 minutes
Add yeast, salt and the oil
Knead it for 10 minutes
If its too sticky put some oil on your hands while kneading
Let it rest in the bowl for a couple hours if it's 20°C, if its colder or warmer wait for the dough to double in size. To be sure that its levitated enough, gently poke the dough with a finger. If its too hard, it needs more time, if it springs back it's ready, if it doesn't spring back, you waited too much (you can still put it in the oven, it'll probably flatten a bit but it's still good to eat)
Set oven at 220°C static with a cup of water on the bottom
Put the dough on a tray then inside the oven in the middle
Let it cook 20 minutes
Remove water, lower temperature to 200°C
Let it cook another 20 minutes
Turn off oven, let the bread cool for at leaat an hour so the crust sets
You have acquired bread, congratulations!
Once you have mastered this simple bread, you can upgrade and go for more complex breads (like sweet breads, breads with weird flours etc).
This bread will be absolutely better than store bought bread, it will be on par with the lower bottom tier of bakeries

With China, what you often see is contract closure, where a joint development fails commercially, but some parties don't get released from the contract and are ransomed by shareholders, told to continue building something which is actively being demolished at the same time.

So it's usually subcontractors building internal walls, lights, balconies, who tokenisticly build something to meet a moot contractual obligation.

This happens in the west as well, notably with scaffolding, demolition, pipe laying. The building is called off, the contractors refuse to hand over valuable building materials for a moot contract, so effectively destroy the materials by building them into a condemned building to punish shareholders for not releasing them from a contract. The funniest one is where scaffolding cranes are erected next to nothing, because they were paid for, the contractor refuses to cancel the contract without compensation, the board of directors calls their bluff, crane is built next to nothing because no deal could be brokered. If you ever see cranes in the city go up and down in one weekend, that's why. Also happens with cement trucks.

A government-supported industrial policy would therefore be essential. This model would allow resource wealth to be transformed into productive industrial capacity. That's just not how farming works at all. It takes a long time to deplete the soil, centuries in fact and only if crop rotation isn't utilized. 75. In primitive societies life is a succession of stages. The needs and purposes of one stage having been fulfilled, there is no particular reluctance about passing on to the next stage. A young man goes through the power process by becoming a hunter, hunting not for sport or for fulfillment but to get meat that is necessary for food. This phase having been successfully passed through, the young man has no reluctance about settling down to the responsibilities of raising a family. It is not the primitive man, who has used his body daily for practical purposes, who fears the deterioration of age, but the modern man, who has never had a practical use for his body beyond walking from his car to his house. comment:
Mufasa and Scar aren’t just relatives. They feel like two gravitational pulls inside the same soul.
Mufasa is vertical. Sky, stars, order, responsibility. He’s the voice that says “remember who you are,” which is really “remember the shape you’re meant to grow into.” He’s not just good, he’s alignment.
Scar is sideways. Shadow, hunger, cleverness without anchor. He doesn’t just want power, he wants to rewrite the rules so power justifies itself. He’s what happens when intelligence detaches from responsibility and starts feeding on it.
Simba gets split between them the moment Mufasa dies. Not just grief, but fragmentation. The “good father” disappears externally, and now it has to be found internally or it rots into guilt.
And that’s where Timon and Pumbaa come in: Hakuna Matata.
Not just “no worries,” but more like a spell of suspension. A pause button on destiny. They don’t guide Simba toward who he’s meant to be, they guide him away from pain. Which, for a while, feels like healing.
They’re not wrong, they’re just… incomplete.
They keep him alive. They keep him soft. They keep him from collapsing inward. But they also keep him from turning back toward the fire.
So Simba grows sideways for a while. Jungle life, bugs, laughter. A kind of emotional exile disguised as freedom.
Then the past calls him back. Simba doesn’t defeat Scar as some external villain alone. He has to confront the part of himself that believed Scar’s lie:
“That everything was your fault.” That’s the real prison. Once that breaks, the fight becomes inevitable. Not heroic in a shiny way, more like a correction snapping into place. The kingdom isn’t just reclaimed physically, it’s rebalanced internally. Rain falls. Fire dies. The land breathes again.
